sentences.text: Mengele is a small item in Auschwitz.
You all make up a big item out of it, because he is called the Angel of Death and all this business.
He was nothing of the sort.
He was a, a normal SS officer who did his job.
That's what he would have said if there had ever been a trial.
And--
Well, I have never seen him hit anybody.
And every time that I know something critical had to be done and when he was asked, he-- his response was fairly favorable.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was part of a machine.
The machine was the thing.
It was a terrible place, what-- the way that it was done.
And it was the ranks of the SS that were beating us and that were these-- this sort of regime of fear.
The officers stood aloof from that.
You know?
They were directing.
I mean, they were guilty.
But they didn't sully their hands, if you know what I mean.
It was a different order of bestiality, if you-- if you'd like to call it.
And Mengele was just one of many.
I mean, there was a whole system going there.
So you-- so your emphasis on Mengele is, is, to me--
Well, I--
Now, I was out of his reach, so to speak.
But-- except for this painting, which got burned.
Anyway, I was running along with this fire extinguisher, spraying people who were running behind me without knowing that it went off.
And this-- And I sprayed this SS man with this fire extinguishing stuff.
[LAUGHS] And he started shouting, sabotage.
You know, that I did it purposely.
And I am to report to him next day and this and that and the other.
And I reported the next day, thinking it's going to be terrible trouble.
You know, I'm going to get a terrible beating.
But somehow the men in the Kleiderkammer got him quiet, quietened down.
What they gave him, I don't know.
But they all did something for me that he-- I had no punishment.
A few days later, I'm called through the camp.
They had a sort of call system that everybody had to repeat the command from the gate.
My number is called to report to the gate.
I report to the gate.
And now, it's man called Buntrach.
He was the Oberscharfuhrer.
He was in charge of the whole camp, you know, from the SS side.
And he says to me, come in, into the guard room, which I'd never be in.
That's when I saw these arms hanging on the wall.
And he opens a desk, one of these standing-up desks, you know.
At the guard post, there was a man who was standing behind the desk.
And he had an opening like a school desk.
But it was a-- like a lecture desk.
He opens this desk.
And he takes out a piece of paper.
And he reads it to me that I-- that I-- there is official permission from Berlin that I can grow my hair.
And he says, I can see you're growing your hair already.
So it's OK.
And he reads on.
And the signature on the piece of paper, he reads the signature.
I'm absolutely flabbergasted.
The signature, Reichsmarschall Goering.
Now, I don't know whether this was a joke.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But this man, Schwarzenberg, applies to Berlin.
The permission comes from Berlin.
And he reads it to me.
And he reads the signature.
No.
He reads the signature.
I mean, you usually have to work out, do I believe him?
Don't I believe him?
What do I do now?
What do I say if he asks me something?
You know, you had to be alive to these things, because every moment was a moment of danger.
It didn't matter what you were, whether you were a privileged prisoner or not.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he sort of bangs at this piece of paper.
He says, don't think that von Schwarzenberg is protecting you.
Because I am in charge here.
And what I say, goes.
[GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] disappear.
So I turned and ran back to, to the camp.
Next thing that happens was three men escape.
And we stand at appell.
The three men that escape is the Blockaltestes of block 11, which is the Sonderkommando, and two of his pals from the Sonderkommando.
They escaped.
This is in August 1944.
And we stand through the night, you know, whilst they're counting and recounting.
And they already know who is missing.
But they're counting and recounting.
And we are standing as punishment because these people are-- people have absconded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, I told you that the camp was riddled with all kinds of trade in vodka and salami and all kinds of contraband.
I mean, in those days, they didn't have drugs.
But if they-- if it had been today, they would have been doing drugs as well.
They would have been doing everything.
Everything was being traded and very high stakes.
I mean, people were coming with big [NON-ENGLISH] of diamonds, you know.
So rings were traded, and-- traded a ring for a loaf of bread or something or a bottle of vodka.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] these three men escape.
And we were eventually let go into our barracks.
And a couple of days later, they are parading these three men back through the camp.
And they've got-- they've got pieces of paper sort of stuck to them, front and back.
Hoorah, hoorah, [GERMAN].
Hooray, hooray, we are here again.
[LAUGHS] Like, [SINGING] we're here again.
So they got terribly beaten up.
Yes.
Because they were block 11.
I was block 15.
And we were counted at appell.
The Kleiderkammer staff at 15 were counted at appell opposite 11, which was block 10.
So as they stood in front of 11, I could see the people from the Sonderkommando that I knew, including Galen.
And I was standing at block 10.
And all this thing went in front of block 11 because they were all block 11 people.
And then, they were so ill from the beating that they went to hospital not in the compound but in Auschwitz.
And then three weeks later, they were hanged.
By this time, it was September.
The gallows was put up with three gallows.
Oh, there was a-- before they were hanged, there was official punishment as well.
That was all in the stalls.
The gallows and the punishment table was in the stalls opposite-- right at the end of the compound at the opposite side from the burnt-out block of this painter.
The punishment table, I have to describe that to you.
I mean, it's something.
They copied it from the Middle Ages.
It was a table with a solid end.
And at the foot of this solid end, there were two footsteps.
And you could put your feet in this way.
And then, you had to twist your feet.
And then you were in a sort of wooden retention so that your feet were not bound but held by this table.
And then, they would stretch you.
Well, you didn't even have to turn your feet, because as they stretched you, the act of stretching made you put your toes forward.
And then, you were captured by the feet.
And you were sort of-- so the upper part of your body was on the table.
Your hands were tied with leather straps to the far side of the table.
And your behind was exposed for beating.
Yes.
They each got 50 strokes.
And they had to count.
And when they lost count at about 25 or so-- they each lost count-- you went back to the number that they last remembered.
And when they got the right number, they resumed the beating.
So they beat them like that, 50 strokes.
Then, they hung them.
They hung them about two or three days.
In the beating, they were already in the block 11.
They were-- they stayed in the block recovering.
And then they-- then they were hung.
And usually, the SS never brought arms into the compound.
But during the hanging, the SS sort of made us all stand on this free ground between the kitchens where the gallows was, was put up.
And the SS was-- stood there armed with submachine guns because these men were Sonderkommando men.
I don't recall, but nothing special.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There was no 'message to Garcia' in what they said.
I mean, this was a big thing going on.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And the intent was to, to frighten us.
I mean, we were already hardened lags, you see.
We had been in the prison for a long time.
But this was an intimidating kind of fear because beating is worse than dying.
In fact, I remember being afraid of getting beaten.
I was afraid of getting beaten.
I wasn't really afraid of getting-- of dying.
I didn't think of what dying was.
It was a concept that I somehow removed from my-- from my thinking.
Well after that, one day-- oh, after that, the Sonderkommando were very busy because the Hungarian transports arrived.
Well, you know, I kept-- I, I kept being life in the Kleiderkammer.
No, nothing at all.
In fact, I was unaffected.
I mean, I had a sinecure job.
And I continued in my sinecure job.
And my hair grew.
And I was quite pleased with that.
[SIGHS] Two more things I want to tell you about Auschwitz, three more perhaps.
And then, perhaps we go on to the next stage.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day, Galen comes along to me.
And he says-- oh yes, there were so many transports that arrived that the railway yards were full of people waiting to be gassed.
The capacity of the system couldn't cope with the number of trains that arrived.
I mean, this is September '44.
So from Hungary, they were sending trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a sort of organizational cartway that was sending people to Auschwitz saying, whilst we still have this factory going, let's kill as many as possible.
And transports arrived all the time.
And the people were sitting on the sidings.
There was nowhere for them to sit.
The road between D lager and C lager was full of people waiting to be killed.
Through these Polish civil workers and through this trade.
We got the news because we were talking to people.
And the SS were talking about it even, I mean, not to me.
But you know, I was with these men of the Kleiderkammer.
And they were informed prisoners because they had the run of the camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the work of the Kleiderkammer, the clothing department.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]
So Galen comes to me.
And he says, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] can you get yourself an errand to go to the Frauenlager and talk to the people in this road, the C road between C and D?
And talk to the people who are sitting next to the wire over the [NON-ENGLISH] lager on the sidings.
Tell them they're going to die.
They're waiting for the gas chambers.
And tell them that when they hear an explosion to throw themselves en masse on the barbed wire and to fell the barbed wire-- you know, make it-- make it fall.
Because our pals will then join us from the [NON-ENGLISH], the rest of the Sonderkommando.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I talked to Harting.
And he arranged it.
So I get an order to go to the Frauenlager and talk to a Blockalteste on block so-and-so that women's detail is to report to Kanada such and such and such and such.
And I have proper orders, you know.
I mean, I didn't have orders.
But that's how it was made up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And we weren't allowed to talk to the prisoners on the ramp.
You know, I already got beaten once to do it.
But I knew something was up.
So I start talking to people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you see, I'm well-dressed like a bellhop [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with this thing.
I mean, to them, it must have been bizarre.
It was bizarre.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And many of the Hungarian Jews were in caftans, you know, sort of-- they were already camped out there for days waiting, really literally waiting to be gassed.
So a few young men take me to a rabbi.
He questions me.
So he asks, Yiddish, you see-- he asks why are you talking Yiddish like, like German, [NON-ENGLISH]?
[NON-ENGLISH] Yiddish is [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
Why is your Yiddish like German?
So I said, look, I come from Moravia.
And I learned Yiddish from Jewish people who came from yeshivot in Poland.
And that's the Yiddish I know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he says to the people around him, this is a [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a, a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, he's trying to tell us something to do, which will cost us our lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he's a German boy.
He's not a Jewish boy.
[LAUGHS]
That's the topsy-turvy world of Auschwitz, you see.
And I go on.
And I talk to some more people.
Of course, they don't believe you.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If I tell you, you're going to walk out of here, and the car is going to come and knock you down, and you're going to die, you know, outside the front door, you'll never believe me.
That's the same in Auschwitz.
It doesn't matter how much you tell them, they won't believe you.
Because it is actually an unbelievable story.
I often think today, you know, is this the reality?
Or was that the reality?
Because it's so unreal to talk about it.
Anyway, next morning there's an explosion.
And within 20 minutes, the Germans have got it under control.
And all that happened was they had some petrol bombs that they somehow smuggled into the compound.
And they filled the building with natural gas.
And that's what exploded.
It's the-- you know, the natural gas was used for burning bodies.
So people talk about all kinds of bomb-making and so on.
That's just imagination.
Anyway, that's the last I saw of Galen.
So-- What happened to him?
He escaped?
Well, in 20 minutes, these 300 men were all gunned down.
And then, the other Sonderkommando men had to burn them.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And now, the situation was that with these dire punishments, which the Germans always meted out to people who were not really responsible, you know, they took-- they took hostage.
They sort of had a method of a hostage punishment.
We were thinking of all-- we were thinking all kinds of things.
But instead, the Germans started dismantling the crematoria.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the rumors about the Russians became stronger and stronger.
And I prepared myself clothes without prisoner markings.
You know, because if any time was to escape, now was the time.
So in December when they said that Auschwitz would be evacuated and whoever wants to leave on the first march can leave, I, in fact, volunteered for the first march.
And I had a blanket, which I organized.
I mean, I was equipped, more or less, you know, to go.
And I had good clothes.
I had the skiing boots that Galen gave me.
Where were you marching to?
Who knows?
I mean, the Germans wouldn't--
Oh, just evacuated.
We were being evacuated.
But the point was to get out of the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had some pieces of gold, pieces of tooth, you know, dug out of teeth, which some Sonderkommando people gave me.
And I was ready to go, you know.
So I volunteered.
And Herting said, look.
Stay.
They're not doing anything to us anymore.
And the crematoria are dismantled.
They are now burying people because the crematoria are dismantled.
You know, all the chambers, the gas chambers, were dismantled.
And he said-- actually, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mean, those three years were such a-- I don't want to call it a nightmare.
But it's bizarre.
Everything is bizarre.
He suddenly says, I was Communist Party Secretary of Belgium.
And Herting is my nom de plume.
And as soon as the Russian comes-- the Russians come, I'll be taken to Moscow.
And you come with me.
And I'll look after you.
And I don't know what gave me this-- because Leo prepared me to try and-- try and run.
That's why I didn't stay with Herting.
But you know what Stalin did to all these people that were in the camps or were somehow in German hands.
None of them survived, you know, the purges on the communist side.
Oh of course, I had no inkling.
What did I know what Moscow looked like?
I mean, he had been to Moscow.
He spoke-- he spoke Russian.
Where did you go?
So I went on this first march.
Just to show you how-- how the Germans were, there's an inspection as we are leaving.
And it's evening.
The light is already fading.
And the SS man sees me in my clothes.
And he says, you've got no markings.
He goes into his cubby-hole, comes out with a pot of red paint, and daubs my clothes with red paint.
That's how they were.
We were being evacuated.
And we could hear the guns.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We could hear the artillery.
And it was Christmas Eve, 1944.
And now started this nightmarish thing that we marched.
And as, as you marched, as you went through the march, you became weaker and weaker because there was no feeding, hardly any rations.
And there was this exhausting marching, even for a lad of 14, 15 by this time.
And we came to some sidings in a place called Leslau.
We were put into trains.
And I arrive in Gross-Rosen.
Oh, and I was marching with a boy, a Polish boy, who was working in the kitchens.
This Polish boy wanted to be friends with me.
And I was sort of rejecting him.
And then, he wanted to trade meat for shoes.
He wanted a pair of shoes.
So I said to Herting, can I select some shoes for a boy in the kitchen?
He wants to give me some meat.
So he says, mm, meat.
OK.
[LAUGHS] So I brought meat and gave this boy a pair of shoes.
But he wanted to be friends.
You know, he wanted to be sort of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was sort of-- I was the prominent.
And he was-- he was dirty, disheveled.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Anyway, now we marched together.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So we were thrown together.
There was-- we get to Gross-Rosen.
And we're not fed.
You know, we get into the camp.
We're counted.
No food.
So he nudges me.
He says, look you're a laufer.
Tell them that you are Mengele's laufer.
I wasn't anymore.
But tell them that you have been Mengele's laufer, and that Mengele knows you and that you'd like to work as a laufer in this camp.
This is in Gross-Rosen now.
And by now, it's January '45.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he talks me into it to go and report to the gate.
And this Scharfuhrer who is in charge of the gate-- same kind of desk, you know-- says, oh, you know Mengele, do you?
So I said, yes.
So he says, well, Mengele's coming this evening.
It's already evening.
But he says, Mengele's due here any moment.
Why don't you sit down, wait for him?
And I thought to myself, god, now I've done it.
You know, I didn't want-- the last person I wanted to meet at this juncture when everybody was running from Auschwitz was Mengele.
